Definition: The first speech test performed in diagnostic audiogram, after pure tone testing is completed. Stimuli can be presented as recorded lists or with live voice. Spondee words are used which are two syllable words with equal stress on both syllables like baseball and toothbrush. Tester is looking for softest volume patient can correctly repeat a word 50% of the time. Each word is presented once and tester manipulates volume to find threshold. Result is a volume level measured in dB HL. This correlates with PTA.
Plain English: Finds the softest level the patient can repeat a word 50% of the time.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Common Customer Questions: Can word lists be downloaded to the audiometer?  Can I play word lists on this audiometer from my CD player? What word lists are available?
Socratic Selling: How would it improve your work flow if SRT lists could be presented from your audiometer automatically?

Definition: The second speech test performed in a diagnostic audiogram, after SRT. Word lists include a carrier phrase followed by a one syllable word the patient needs to repeat. Ex. "Say the word cup" is the presentation and the patient needs to say "cup". The intensity level of the presentation does not change during testing. The presentation level is typically determined by the SRT score. Most common word lists are the NU6 and CID W22. Tester marks response as correct or incorrect and may present 25-50 words for each ear. Result is a % correct along with what dB HL level was used. Typically performed in each ear separately, but may be done binaurally.
Plain English: Longer word list than SRT and intensity doesn't change.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Common Customer Questions: Can word lists be downloaded to the audiometer?  Can I play word lists on this audiometer from my CD player? What word lists are available? Can masking be performed?
Socratic Selling: How would it improve your work flow if SRT lists could be presented from your audiometer automatically?

Definition: An impression is taken of the ear if clinician is ordering an ear piece for patient to connect to hearing aid, or could be for a hearing protection device. Impression material is a putty like substance that hardens in the ear after a few minutes and is removed.
Plain English: The mold made of the patients ear to create a custome ear piece
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Common Customer Questions: We waste time waiting for our earmold impressions to be shipped to the manufacturer, made, and then shipped back - do you have any solutions?
Socratic Selling: What would it mean for your clinic efficiency if you could scan your EMIs and send directly to the manufacturer the same day?

Definition: A custom made ear piece used with hearing aids or as hearing protection
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: Objective tests that verify the settings of hearing aids specific to the patient's hearing loss and ear anatomy. Specialized equipment is required. Typical set up includes a probe tube placed in patient's ear along with hearing aid. Calibrated auditory stimuli are presented in front of patient while equipment measures the output of the hearing aid in the patients ear. Considered the "gold standard" but not performed by all practitioners.
Plain English: The best way to confirm the hearing aid is performing exactly how it needs to.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Related Terms: RECD, WRECD, Test Box, Speechmapping, Otoscopy
Common Customer Questions: My hearing aid return rate is low, why should I start doing REM?  I know REM is the gold standard, and I want to do what's best for my patients, but aren't all REM units extremely expensive?
Socratic Selling: How might it impact your patients perspective of their care if they know you are offering the gold standard for hearing aid verification?

Definition: Air Conduction - Testing via inserts, headphones, or speakers. Sound travels through outer, middle, and inner ear.  Bone Conduction - Testing via bone oscillator. Sound bypasses outer and middle ear and tests directly to inner ear.   Performing both tests helps to identify the type of hearing loss.
Plain English: How is the sound being presented to the patient and what parts of the hearing pathway is it assessing?
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital, Schools, Pediatricians
Common Customer Questions: I am a school nurse doing annual hearing screenings- do we need to be performing bone conduction testing?  Why should I perform both air and bone conduction testing if it takes longer?
Socratic Selling: How might it benefit your patients if you had bone conduction available when you need it?

Definition: Testing hearing via headphones, inserts, or soundfield speakers. Sound travels through outer, middle, and inner ear.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital, Schools, Pediatricians
Related Equipment: IP30 Inserts, DD45 headphones, TDH headphones, DD65 v2 headphones, Soundfield speakers
Common Customer Questions: What air conduction transducers are available with this audiometer?

Definition: Testing hearing via bone oscillator which is worn on a headband. Sound bypasses outer and middle ear and tests directly to inner ear. This is needed for identification of conductive hearing loss.
Plain English: Vibrating piece worn on headband and placed against patients head to test inner ear
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Common Customer Questions: Does this audiometer have bone conduction?  Can I add bone conduction to this audiometer later? Can this ABR system perform bone conduction ABR?

Definition: Difference between the sound pressure level (SPL) in a patients ear and the SPL in a standardized 2cc coupler. Without this measurement the hearing aid will be set based on the 2cc coupler volume and assume that the patients ear is exactly that size. In reality there are big differences between every persons ear canal volume. Without this measurement a hearing aid could be over amplifying or underamplifying. This is especially important for pediatrics. To measure, a probe tube is placed in patients ear with hearing aid and a calibrated stimuli is presented while the output is measured near the eardrum. Then the same hearing aid is placed in a test box and attached to a 2cc coupler while a sound is played and output is measured in the coupler. The equipment calculates the difference and applies the newly measured RECD to hearing aid targets.
Plain English: Measures the differences between hearing aid amplification recorded in the ear canal and in a 2cc coupler and uses that to make more precise hearing aid settings.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Related Equipment: Verifit, Noah Link, Avant REM, AWRC, Affinity Compact
Common Customer Questions: Why should I perform RECD testing when I already have no extra time with patients?
Socratic Selling: What would it mean for your efficiency if RECD measures could be performed faster? How beneficial would it be to you if your audiometric data and RECD data could instantly transfer back and forth between the test equipment and your computer?

Full Name: Wideband Real Ear to Coupler Difference
Definition: A more modern expanded version of RECD. Uses a smaller coupler size of 0.4cc compared to the 2cc coupler used in RECD. Stimuli includes extended frequencies compared to RECD.
Plain English: An updated version of RECD
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Common Customer Questions: Whats the difference between RECD and WRECD?
Socratic Selling: Hearing aid technology now offers extended frequency ranges- how might it help your patients hearing aid outcomes if your REM system kept up with these changes?

Definition: A test within hearing aid verification equipment. Patient is set up with probe tube in ear along with hearing aid. Stimuli is calibrated speech signals. A graph is displayed in real time of how hearing aids perform to speech signals at different intensity levels. Clinician can make realt time changes to hearing aid programming in response to speechmapping results.
Plain English: Hearing aid verification with calibrated speech signals and real time results to aid in fast hearing aid adjustments
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Related Terms: RECD, WRECD, Test Box, Speechmapping, Otoscopy
Related Equipment: Verifit, Affinity Compact, Avant REM, AWRC
Common Customer Questions: Why should I do Speechmapping when I can just talk to the patient and ask them how the hearing aids sound? Can my hearing aid programming software interface with the REM equipments speechmapping?
Socratic Selling: How would it improve your work flow if speechmapping results could overlay onto the hearing aid programming screen while you made adjustments?

Definition: Behavioral hearing test typically used for patients 9 months - 3 years. Video or toy VRA devices are mounted to the left and right of patient. Tester conditions them to respond to sound by turning to the direction of the sound by rewarding them with a visual stimuli from VRA device.
Plain English: Video screens and/or toy boxes mounted in a sound booth to aid in audiometry with young children.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, Hospital, School
Related Equipment: Flex Halo VRA, Flex Video VRA, Flex Toy, Conditioned Play Innovations VRA Pro Stealth, Conditioned Play Innovations VRA Go
Common Customer Questions: Can this VRA system interface with my audiometer? Can I add my own video files to the video VRA?
Socratic Selling: How might it improve your work flow if your VRA could be controlled by your audiometer? How would it benefit your test outcomes if you had more variety of video stimuli in your VRA?

Definition: Typically used for patients 3 - 5 years, or until they can participate in a traditional hearing test. Tester conditions patient to respond to auditory stimuli by completing a game-like task (ex. put the ball in the bucket when you hear a beep). Requires one tester to be in the booth with patient.
Plain English: Training patient to response to stimuli by completing a fun task each time they hear the stimuli.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, Hospital, School
Common Customer Questions: Do you have an audiometer that would make CPA easier for a one tester system where I have to be in the booth with the patient?

Definition: Commonly used acronym for the GSI Audiostar Pro audiometer
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: Commonly used acronym for the GSI Tympstar Pro tympanometer
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: A diagnostic test measuring the brainstems response to auditory stimuli. Patient is set up with electrodes on head and auditory stimuli is presented either via air or bone conduction transducer. Wave forms are produced on computer screen and clinician analyzes and marks waveforms. Patient needs to be still and quiet for accurate recordings. Results can reveal estimated hearing levels along with identifying issues beyond the cochlea. Specialized equipment is needed. Useful test for infants and other patients who cannot participate in behavioral hearing test.
Plain English: A specialized test that can tell us about a patients hearing while they are asleep
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital
Common Customer Questions: We want to begin offering ABR testing but we need to be able to take the equipment to different parts of the hospital- is that possible?
Socratic Selling: How would it help you if you had an ABR system that was more resistant to electrical noise and did not require the patient to be asleep?

Definition: Term describing auditory evoked potential tests wherein a response from central auditory system is measured via electrodes in response to auditory stimuli
Plain English: Test that measures responses from the central auditory system in response to sound
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: A series of tests that record eye movement and look for nystagmus. Patient wears goggles with camers and a visual stimuli is presented. Tests the vestibular system.
Plain English: Balance testing with goggles.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital, PT
Related Equipment: Interacoustics Visual Eyes, Rotary Chair, Virtualis, TRV Chair, AirFx, Aquastim

Definition: Radio Frequency

A movement sensor added to VNG goggles. Enables clinician to track head movement and position and identify which semicircular canal is being tested. Adds ability to perform additional VNG tests like Dynamic Visual Acuity and Gaze Stabilization.
Plain English: Movement tracker attached to VNG goggles.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital, PT
Common Customer Questions: What is the benefit of getting the IMU? What tests come with this package? Can I take the IMU on and off when needed?

Definition: Vestibular test used to assess the vestibulo-ocular reflex. Patient wears goggles and eye movements are recorded along with head movements. Clinician can quantify the VOR and detect both covert and overt saccades.
Plain English: Evaluates how well inner ear and brain work together to keep eyes focused on target while head is moving.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital, PT
Common Customer Questions: I don't have the budget for a full VNG unit, what is something else I could purchase to perform some type of assessment on the vestibular system and make referrals if needed?

Definition: A non-linguistically based hearing in noise test that can predict the patients ability to hear in noise when wearing hearing aids. First a complete hearing test is needed. The ACT test runs through the audiometer using the same air conduction transducers as the typical hearing test. The ACT test uses data from the pure tone thresholds to select a starting intensity level. The patient hears noise and a siren signal. They press the patient response button when they hear the siren signal. The clinician controls the test. Results provide a guide for hearing aid settings.
Plain English: A hearing in noise test
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Related Equipment: Affinity Compact, Equinox Evo, Audiostar Pro, Pello, A2D+, ARC, AWRC
Common Customer Questions: Can the ACT be performed on a non-English speaker? What data points are needed to perform the ACT test? Does my audiometer have ACT and if not can I add it?

Definition: Standards applied to audiology equipment and testing

Tests that evaluate the patients ability to hear speech in the presence of background noise. May be used to validate hearing aid benefit, assessing for auditory processing disorders, or assessing cochlear implant candidacy
Plain English: Speech tests that also have noise in the background
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital
Common Customer Questions: Why do I need a 2 channel audiometer to do certain speech in noise tests? Does the audiometer already have these SIN tests I need, or can they be added?

Definition: Anatomical term referring to something on the same side of the body. If an ipsilateral reflex is being performed in the right ear, it is measuring a right ear response
Plain English: Same side
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: Anatomical term referring to something on the opposite side of the body. If an contralateral reflex is being performed in the right ear, it is measuring a left ear response
Plain English: Opposite side
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: Measurement unit for frequency. Ex 1000 Hz
Plain English: Measurement unit for volume

Definition: Unit of frequency equal to 1000 Hz. 1000 Hz = 1 kHz

Also known as Auditory Processing Disorder (APD). A condition characterized by problems with neural processing of auditory information. The outer, middle, and inner ear may function normally but an anomaly in the central auditory system could be present resulting in processing problems. Can be congenital or acquired - so present at any age. Specialized speech testing is required to diagnose along with a 2 channel audiometer and soundfield speakers.
Plain English: A processing problem wherein hearing might be normal but ability to process sounds/speech is not.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital, School

Definition: Customer may use "HF" to refer to the 1000 Hz signal in tympanometry.
A specific SIN test commonly used in pediatrics and cochlear implant users
Measures pure tone thresholds with a special masking noise called TEN. Identifies cochlear dead regions.
Plain English: Specialized test that identifies areas in the cochlea that are not functioning
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: A speech test which is composed of sentences presented to a patient that they must repeat. Measures speech perception ability. Often used with cochlear implant candidates or users. Requires access to specialized word list
Plain English: Speech perception test for CI users
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: A surgically placed medical device that bypasses outer, middle, and inner ear to stimulate the auditory nerve directly. Used for patients with hearing loss that is too severe for a traditional hearing aid.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: An amplification device to assist patients with hearing loss. Many different styles exist.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Related Terms: REM, RECD, WRECD, Speechmapping, SIN, ACT
Related Equipment: Verifit, Axiom, Affinity Compact, AWRC, Avant REM, Redux, 3Shape Scanner, Video Otoscope

Definition: A licensed hearing healthcare professional that can perform hearing evaluations and hearing aid fittings/programming. Unlike an audiologist, they do not have a doctoral degree and cannot diagnose auditory conditions.
Potential Customers: Hearing Aid Dispenser

Definition: An objective test used to assist in cochlear implant programming. The cochlear implant is worn and stimulated by the cochlear implant software while an ear probe from a tympanometer is placed in the ipsilateral or contralateral ear recording stapedial reflex activity that occurs in response to the cochlear implant stimulation. The eSRT measurements are highly coorelated to where the upper levels of the CI should be set to prevent overstimulation. This test is especially useful in pediatrics.
Plain English: A test that uses a tympanometer while audiologist presents sound through the cochlear implant
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: An immitance recording that tests the response of the eardrum to a wide range of frequencies, rather than traditional audiometry than uses one frequency like 226 Hz. Requires a compatible tympanometer and WBT license. Performed in the same way as a traditional tympanogram. Improves ability to diagnose many middle ear disorders.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital
Common Customer Questions: I thought WBT was really only used in research, why should I add it? Will I still get the typical tympanometry tracing I am used to seeing?
Socratic Selling: What would it mean for you if one immitance test could provide the tympanogram you're used to but much more information about the middle ear?

Definition: Sounds generated by normally functioning cochleas in response to external auditory stimuli, somewhat like an echo. A probe tip is placed in the ear and presents sounds while also measuring for the emissions coming from the cochlea. The emissions are very quiet so it is important that the patient and test environment is very quiet. OAE results are objective and can detect cochlear function. May be used as a hearing screening tool or as a diagnostic test to tell the clinician more about the entire auditory system.
Plain English: A test of cochlear function
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital, Schools, Pediatricians
Related Equipment: Corti, Titan, Vivosonic Integrity, Eclipse, Eroscan, Lyra, Audera pro, Otoread, Otoport Pro, Echoport, Otocheck LE

Definition: A specific OAE test named for the stimuli presented to the ear. Most commonly used OAE test.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital, Schools, Pediatricians
Related Equipment: Corti, Titan, Vivosonic Integrity, Eclipse, Eroscan, Lyra, Audera pro, Otoread, Otoport Pro, Echoport, Otocheck LE

Definition: A specific OAE test named for the stimuli presented to the ear. Typically less common than DPOAE but may be preferred by clinician or required for protocol.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital, Schools, Pediatricians
Related Equipment: Corti, Titan, Vivosonic Integrity, Eclipse, Eroscan, Lyra, Audera pro, Otoread, Otoport Pro, Echoport, Otocheck LE

Definition: Abbreviation clinician may use for "diagnostic"
An automated screening version of an ABR that is typically used in newborn hearing screenings
Potential Customers: Hospital
Related Equipment: Vivosonic Integrity, Otoport Pro, Novus, OtoNova Pro

Definition: An automated screening version of an ABR that is typically used in newborn hearing screenings. Term for AABR used by Interacoustics.
Potential Customers: Hospital

Definition: A specific group of word lists used for WRS testing. Typically automatically included in a newer audiometer with the ability to present recorded word lists.
Acronym used to describe equipment that is no longer manufactured or supported by manufacturer. EOL letters for specific equipment are available in Modus.

A specialized physician who diagnoses and treats conditions of the ears, nose, and throat. Often working with audiologists to obtain hearing tests. Certain tests may be requested by an audiologist based on what their ENT typically asks them to perform (ex. ETD)
Statistical calculation used in AEP testing to determine what is a response and what is just noise or artifact. Used as a validity measure.
The level of speech relative to the level of background noise

Also known as AMLR.  AEP test that specifically tells clinician about auditory cortex. Performed using a similar procedure to an ABR but stimuli and recording window differ. Waveforms will appear differently than ABR and are labeled differently. May be used in conjunction with an ABR to assess more central function.
Potential Customers: Audiology clinic, Hospital, VA, ENT

Definition: A specialized diagnostic test that measures the electrical potential of the cochlea. Can diagnose certain vestibular and auditory conditions. Unique to this test, a special electrode is placed on the eardrum or deep into the ear canal.
Potential Customers: Audiology clinic, Hospital, VA, ENT

Definition: Term referring to the time the clinician has with a patient to discuss their test results and treatment.

Describes the inspection of the ear canal and tympanic membrane with an otoscope or video otoscope. Should be performed prior to hearing assessments. Can help clinician identify anomalies in the outer ear and/or tympanic membrane. May reveal presence of fluid behind the TM, but not always visible with otoscopy.
Related Equipment: MedRx Video Otoscope, GSI Video Otoscope

Definition: Tester presents speech stimuli to patient at different volumes to look for volume level the patient can detect the presence of speech 50% of the time. Correlates with pure tone average. Unlike SRT, which assesses patients speech perception by having them repeat back the word, the SDT is just looking at whether or not a word was heard.
Plain English: Whats the quietest level patient can detect speech.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser

Definition: Hospital system exclusive to veterans.

Describes the manner in which a patient was tested - either wiithout their amplification device or without it. Aided testing may be performed to validate hearing aid settings. True hearing aid verification requires REM.

Describes a noise signal presented during a hearing test to one ear, while testing the other. Needed when one ear hears better than the other - the better ear will be presented with masking while the test stimuli is presented to the poorer ear, this way the clinician knows they are recording responses heard by the poorer ear. Without masking, the signal presented to the poorer ear may be loud enough to be heard by the better ear and those responses would not accurately show the hearing of the poorer ear.
Plain English: A method to block out the non test ear and test responses from the test ear

Definition: Describes a method of performing speech testing by the clinician speaking the word lists to the patient in real time, rather than using a recording word list. Clinician can monitor the output of their voice on a VU meter to ensure they arent presenting to loudly or quietly, but ultimately not as standardized as a recorded list.
Anatomical name for the eardrum.
Related Equipment: MedRx Video Otoscope, GSI Video Otoscope

Definition: A hearing aid verification component wherein hearing aid output can be measured without the patient present. A variety of tests can be performed in the test box.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Related Equipment: Axiom, Verifit, Affinity Compact, Avant HIT Box

Definition: A hearing aid verification component wherein hearing aid output can be measured without the patient present. A variety of tests can be performed in the test box.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, Hearing Aid Dispenser, ENT, VA, Hospital
Related Equipment: Axiom, Verifit, Affinity Compact, Avant HIT Box

Definition: Measurement assessing the lowest intensity level that triggers an involuntary contraction in the middle ear of the stapedius muscle. Can be performed as ipsilateral or contralateral measurement. Elevated or absent threshold may suggest hearing loss or anomaly along the reflex pathway. Performed using a tympanometer that is equipped with reflex testing.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital
Related Equipment: Tympstar Pro, Titan, AT235, TouchTymp, EasyTymp, Otowave

Definition: Medical condition where the Eustachian Tube does not function properly to drain fluid and equalize the pressure in the middle ear. Patient with ETD may experience ear pain, ear popping, and reduced hearing. To diagnose, a Eustachian Tube Function (ETF) test is performed.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: Test performed to diagnose Eustachian Tube Dysfunction. ETF test is performed with a compatible tympanometer and analyzed by ENT and audiologist. Test is similar to tympanometry but requires additional recordings and steps.
Potential Customers: Audiology Clinic, ENT, VA, Hospital

Definition: System used by clinic to store patient data. Examples include EPIC and Cerner. Important to know if customer wants to transfer test results from equipment to system.
Measurement recorded via tympanometry. Especially important value when assessing presence/absence of a TM perforation or when assessing whether an ear tube is still in place and functioning.
Related Equipment: Tympstar Pro, Titan, AT235, TouchTymp, EasyTymp, Otowave
